terms.definition: unbroken — said of a kāyotsarga that has not been interrupted.
Foods forbidden to eat; the text enumerates the standard twenty-two (e.g. the five udumbara fruits, honey, butter, items 'gone bad in taste').
Fearlessness; the gift of safety the Jinas bestow ('abhayakara').
A binding resolve or self-imposed restraint, held so long as conditions (not seized, not deceived, no disease) permit.
Struck or knocked — the first and lightest of the ten modes of injury listed in verse 7.
False accusation; imputing a fault to someone that they do not have.
Right conduct, here in five domains — knowledge (jñāna), faith (darśana), character (cāritra), austerity (tapa) and energy (vīrya) — whose lapses are confessed after the twelve vows.
The head and exemplar of the monastic order who upholds and transmits right conduct; the third Parameṣṭhin (v.3).
A monastic preceptor and head of an order; '-sūri' is the title suffixed to such names.
'To take up, honor, adopt' — the verb governing the twenty-five virtues to be embraced.
Taking what has not been given; theft.
The 'two-and-a-half continents' — the region of the Jain universe where human life and thus monks can exist.
The 'two-and-a-half continents' — the region of the middle world inhabited by human beings, hence by the four-fold Saṅgha.
A declared, permitted exception within a vow that does not break it (e.g., speaking with fellow vow-keepers or gurus).
Non-violence; refraining from harm to any living being — the first pillar of dharma.
The 'ageless, deathless place' — mokṣa, final liberation; the unobstructed goal of v.4.
'The unconquered'; Ajitanātha, the 2nd Tīrthaṅkara — and, by word-play, the quality of invincibility itself.
The 2nd Tīrthaṅkara of this age; golden-hued, born at Ayodhyā (Kosalā) to King Jitaśatru, with the elephant as emblem.
Spotless, stainless, without blemish — qualifying the śīla of the great satīs.
Nectar of immortality; in verse 3 a metaphor for the life-giving grace residing in Gautamasvāmī.
The command or teaching of the Jina (Skt. ājñā); to be accepted as authoritative.
Inadvertence; acting without awareness — one of the standard āgāras.
Graded degrees of fault named in the closing summary: from a mere transgressive impulse (atikrama) and overstepping (vyatikrama) up to outright wrong conduct (anāchāra).
Bodies that house infinitely many souls (mainly tubers and roots); the text lists thirty-two such items to be avoided as food.
Unattached, dependent on no single support or household (Prakrit aṇissiya).
The sūtra recited right after Tassa Uttarī, listing the involuntary bodily acts (breathing, coughing, etc.) that are permitted and do not break the kāyotsarga.
The five 'lesser vows' — limited forms (suited to lay life) of non-violence, truth, non-stealing, chastity, and non-possession.
Systematic exposition or commentary of a scriptural text; here especially of the Kālika-śruta.
Water-bodied beings (one-sensed); 7 lakh forms.
'I cast off the self' — renouncing the wrong-doing self and its attachments for the period of the vow.
Celestial nymphs; in v.6 the 'accharagaṇa' (host of apsarases) join the Saṅgha in praising the Jina.
Arhat — the 'foe-destroyer', one who has annihilated the inner enemies (passions) and attained omniscience; the awakened, living, fully-enlightened teacher.
An omniscient victor who has destroyed the inner enemies (passions) and, while still embodied, expounds the path; the first Parameṣṭhin (v.1).
Health — here both bodily wellbeing and, by extension, the soul's 'health' of liberation, asked for in v.6.
Disrespect, irreverence, or violation of decorum toward what is sacred (the guru's seat, study-implements, the vow itself).
The 'eight-petalled lotus' visualized in the body as the seat of the meditated tattva (v.5).
The eight great miraculous attributes (e.g. aśoka tree, divine throne, halo, drum) that accompany a Tīrthaṅkara; named among the Jina's standard adornments.
The eight divine attributes manifesting around an Arhat — including the three white umbrellas (ātapatra-traya), the chowries (cāmara), and the celestial drum (dundubhi) named in v.13.
The 'eight mothers of the doctrine': the five samitis plus the three guptis, which together nurture and protect right conduct.
the 'eighteen-syllable' mantra said in v.23 to lie hidden in the middle of the stotra, meditated on by those who know it.
'One hundred and eight' (lit. 'a hundred plus eight'); here the auspicious count of tīrthas garlanded in the hymn.
A 'transgression' — a partial or subtle violation that strains a vow without wholly breaking it; each of the twelve vows has five. (Note: this book spells it અતિયાર, not અતિચાર.)
The supernatural excellences or marks of perfection of a Tīrthaṅkara (v.2 calls Pārśva endowed with crores of pure atiśayas).
Past, present and future — the three time-frames in which the hymn salutes the successive sets of 24 Tīrthaṅkaras.
The small consecrated space immediately around the guru; the disciple asks permission (aṇujāṇaha) before entering it.
A reverential turning gesture of the joined hands, moving from the guru back to one's own forehead, made at fixed words.
An obligatory daily duty; here also the 25 'essentials' (sthāna) said to constitute a correct vandana.
unviolated, undamaged — free of fault or transgression.
Sanskrit ācārya; the head and spiritual preceptor of a monastic community, master of conduct and doctrine.
The 'twelve vows' of the Jain householder: five aṇuvrata, three guṇavrata, and four śikṣāvrata.
The thirty-two faults of sāmāyika — ten of mind, ten of speech, twelve of body — confessed in Sūtra item (7).
Two-, three-, and four-sensed beings (2 lakh forms each), adding taste, smell, and sight in turn.
'Auspiciousness!' / 'May there be welfare!' — the recurring benedictory refrain that opens the praise of Vīra and of the Saṅgha.
dizziness or giddiness.
'O reverend one' — a respectful vocative addressing the guru (or, in the guru's absence, the established sthāpanā), before whom the vow is declared.
Bharata, the continent/region of our world where Tīrthaṅkaras arise and the tīrtha endures.
Carefulness of speech; one of the five samitis (regulations of conduct).
Inner state / disposition; the mental scope of the vow, held to one's capacity and free of attachment and aversion.
The 'ocean of worldly existence' (saṁsāra); the Jina and his tīrthas are the boat, raft and helmsman by which it is crossed.
The 'mansion-dwelling' gods of the lower world (asurakumāra, nāgakumāra, etc.) whose realms hold countless Jina-temples.
A bhavya soul — one capable of liberation; here a vocative, 'O fortunate one'.
The remaining (aghātī) karmas that sustain embodied existence; their exhaustion precedes final liberation (v.5).
Fear/danger; v.9 lists the canonical great fears that remembering Pārśva's name dissolves.
'fear-remover'; the epithet that gives the stotra its popular name.
Bee; the central simile for the mendicant who gathers gently without harm.
A 'seed' syllable (e.g. hrīṃ, oṃ, kṣipa, svāhā) that concentrates a mantra's power; several appear verbatim in vv.3–4, 7.
The seed of enlightenment / attainment of right faith; what the closing verse prays for 'birth after birth'.
'Attainment of enlightenment'; the gift of right insight/awakening petitioned in v.6 (Prakrit bohi-lābha).
A 'point' or spoken item of contemplation; here, the fifty resolutions (pacāsa bol).
The 'aperture of brahman' at the crown of the head, the upper point of the inner meditation-channel (v.5).
A Jain shrine or temple housing a Jina image; the hymn counts caityas across every region of the cosmos.
A liturgical act of bowing to the Jina through his caityas (images and temples); here an extended (bṛhat) one.
A universal emperor possessing fourteen jewels and nine treasures; the worldly sovereignty Śāntinātha renounced.
'the pair of feet' (Skt. caraṇa-yugala); the Jina's feet as the object of refuge, repeated across the danger-verses.
Moon — emblem of Vīra's serene face and the ornament of the sky (nabhas).
Right conduct; the 'unbroken walls' (akhaṇḍa-cāritra) of the Saṅgha-city.
'Universal monarch of the four quarters' — here a metaphor: the Arihanta is the sovereign wheel-turner of the Dharma, dharma-vara-cāuraṁta-cakkavaṭṭi.
The fourteen-rajju vertical span of the inhabited universe (loka), through which karmically bound souls transmigrate.
'Praise of the twenty-four'; the formal name of this hymn and of the Second Āvaśyaka it anchors.
A set of twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras belonging to one age of one region (Sanskrit caturviṁśatikā).
The fourteen-rajju cosmos — the full vertical extent of the Jain universe across which all jīva-yoni are distributed.
Eighty-four lakh (8.4 million) — the traditional total number of jīva-yoni.
Prakrit name of the Second Āvaśyaka — the obligatory recitation honouring the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras.
A set of twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras; the verse salutes the past (atīta), future (anāgata), and present (vartamāna) sets.
The state of being veiled by destructive karmas; 'viaṭṭa-chaüma' (v.8) means one who has cast off this veil — possessing unobstructed supreme knowledge and vision (kevala-jñāna and kevala-darśana).
The thirty-six virtues that together define a true guru (18 + 18 across the two verses).
The 'six ways' in which the sāmāyika is undertaken — not doing, not causing, not approving wrongful acts, across mind, speech and body.
The wish-fulfilling gem; with the wish-tree (suratarū) and wish-cow, a stock image for the boundless boon of meeting the Jina.
An appended, explanatory addendum; here the four closing lines (vv.6–9) that praise the fruit of the five salutations.
A demoness/witch, another of the malign beings whose harm is averted by remembering the Jina-pair (v.6).
The four-fold dharma: charity, ethical conduct, austerity, and inner disposition/intent.
Tamed, self-controlled; one who has mastered the senses.
The 10th lay vow: voluntarily narrowing, for a set time, the boundaries of one's movement and activity already limited by the 6th vow (dig-vrata), thereby reducing the scope for harm.
Celestial beings and infernal (hell) beings; 4 lakh forms each in this enumeration.
Celestial women / goddesses who venerate the Jina's feet with song and dance (vv.26–31).
Object of devotion/refuge; in this verse the Parameṣṭhins, not worldly gods, are taken as one's true 'deity'.
The nāga-king (serpent lord) who, in tradition, shelters and protects Pārśvanātha — background to the hymn's serpent/poison imagery.
The teaching and right order proclaimed by the Jina; in v.7 the Arihanta is its giver, teacher, leader, charioteer (sārathi), and four-quartered universal monarch (cakravartin).
The sixth lay vow, fixing outer limits on travel in the directions; the Deśāvagāśika contracts these limits further for a short time.
Renunciation; formal entry into the ascetic life in pursuit of liberation (v.3).
Substance / material things; here, the physical items a practitioner may keep during the vow, only as previously resolved.
Tree; whose flowers (puṣpa) the bee visits — root of the chapter-name Drumapuṣpikā.
Pained directly / caused to be pained / approved the paining of — the threefold agency (kṛta–kārita–anumodita) covered by the confession.
Calamity, misfortune, evil/sin; the closing prayer asks Vīra for the 'destruction of all duritas' (savva-duriyāṇaṁ khaya).
'Two-fold by three means' — the scope formula: abstaining from both doing and causing to be done (two-fold), through mind, speech, and body (three means).
'Twelve turnings' — the twelve ritual hand-movements (āvarta) made across the paired recitation, which name this salutation.
Aversion or hatred; the push against what is unpleasant. With rāga it forms the twin root of karmic bondage.
'By two ways and by three means' — the formula defining the reach of the renunciation: two ways = doing (karaṇa) and causing to be done (kārāpaṇa); three means = mind, speech, body.
One-sensed beings, possessing only touch: the earth-, water-, fire-, air-, and plant-bodied jīvas.
Beings classified by sense-faculties: one-sensed (e.g. plants, earth-bodies) up to five-sensed (e.g. humans, animals).
The disciplined seeking/inspection of alms (here dāna-bhatta-esaṇā, seeking of alms-food).
A monastic order or lineage-branch within Śvetāmbara Jainism.
The reigning head of the gaccha, invoked by the incumbent's name.
Lord of elephants — emblem of Vīra's majestic gait and the ornament of land (sthala).
'Going and coming' — movement to and from the place of ritual, the occasion whose faults this sutra confesses.
Going and coming; movement to and fro, the occasion of the faults confessed.
A monastic group or school descending from a particular teacher.
A chief disciple of a Tīrthaṅkara who systematizes his teaching; Mahāvīra had eleven, from Indrabhūti to Prabhāsa (vv.22–23).
Self-indulgent pride: ṛddhi- (in wealth/status), rasa- (in taste), and sātā- (in comfort) gārava.
Censure before the guru — openly condemning the fault in the presence of one's teacher; 'garihāmi' = 'I condemn (it before the guru).'
'I reproach (myself before the guru)' — outward acknowledgement of faults in the teacher's presence.
A Prakrit verse in the āryā-type metre; the poet calls his praise a 'gāhā-juala' (pair of gāthās) in v.3.
Gautamasvāmī (Indrabhūti Gautama), the chief gaṇadhara of Mahāvīra, famed for his labdhis and revered as a giver of blessings.
The four 'destructive' karmas that obscure knowledge, perception, energy, and bring delusion; their destruction yields omniscience (v.4).
Clan or family-line; teachers are identified by gotra (e.g. Kāśyapa, Kauśika, Hārita).
A planetary/astral influence held to cause harm; one of the troubles that 'subside' in v.2.
A virtue or distinguishing quality; each rank of being is named by the fixed count of its gunas.
A 'host of virtues'; the exemplars are described as joined to (saṁyukta) such virtue-multitudes (v.7).
The three 'merit-enhancing vows' that reinforce the aṇuvratas: directional limits, limits on use/enjoyment, and avoidance of purposeless harm.
Restraint or guarding; the three guptis are control of mind, speech and body. 'Guttidharo' = bearer of these guards.
The act of invoking and 'installing' the guru at the start of the observance — the purpose of this sutra.
A celestial king; thirty-two Indras perform the birth-bath of the Jinas (v.2).
Pertaining to the īryā-patha, the 'path of movement'; the confession for faults incurred while walking or moving.
The immediately preceding sūtra, a confession (micchāmi dukkaḍaṁ) for harm done to living beings while moving about — the fault that Tassa Uttarī now resolves to purify.
Carefulness in walking, so as not to harm beings underfoot; the samiti most directly engaged by movement on the road.
Jambūsvāmī, traditionally the last person of this era to attain kevala-jñāna (omniscience); remembered in verse 2.
'For as long as I attend upon …' — the clause fixing the vow's duration; in the ordinary sāmāyika the object is 'niyama', here it is the 'deśāvagāśika' observance.
'as long as … until then' — the conditional pair that brackets the duration of the vow (vv. 4–5).
'For as long as (I am bound by) the rule' — the clause fixing the duration of the vow to the period of the sāmāyika.
Sanskrit yatanā — conscientious carefulness; the prudent exemption a layperson allows for subtle, unavoidable harm in necessary activity, distinct from intentional violation.
'Victor' — one who has conquered the inner passions; a synonym for the Tīrthaṅkaras (intensified as jinavara, 'best of Jinas').
A 'conqueror' / 'ford-maker' — a fully awakened teacher who founds the path; twenty-four arise in each age of a region.
A Jina-temple or shrine (jinhar, jinabhavana, jinālaya), enumerated in vast numbers throughout the cosmos.
An image or icon of a Jina; the hymn counts crores of them across the worlds (paḍimā).
'Moon among the Jinas', a laudatory epithet for Pārśvanātha in the final verse.
The renowned Pārśvanātha pilgrimage site (Jīrāvalā, Sirohī, Rājasthān) after which this form of the Lord is named.
A living being or soul; in Jain thought possessed even by earth, water, fire, air, and plant bodies.
The aggregate or classes of living beings; the title-theme of the sūtra.
A birth-form or womb — a distinct kind of physical situation into which a soul can be born; counted as 84 lakh in total.
Deprived of life, killed — the gravest mode of injury, the culmination of verse 7.
Knowledge; the Nandī's central subject, classed as five — mati (abhinibodhika), śruta, avadhi, manaḥparyaya and kevala.
Implements and materials dedicated to sacred knowledge — manuscripts, writing-boards, stands, rosaries — disrespect to which is confessed.
'Time' — the duration of the vow, here a muhūrta up to the measure of one's resolve.
The crowning concluding stanza that 'pours' a benediction and names the gaccha, the presiding ācārya and the author.
The class of canonical texts studied only at fixed (kālika) times; the body of teaching the named masters preserved.
The tiered heavens of the upper world (Saudharma, Īśāna … up to the Anuttaras) surveyed in dhāla 6.
The kalpa-vṛkṣa, the wish-granting celestial tree; paired with the cintāmaṇi as something right faith surpasses.
the demon (asura) whose storm-and-flood assault Pārśva endured without breaking his meditation (v.22).
Doing oneself and causing another to do; 'na karemi, na kāravemi' = 'I neither do nor cause to be done.' (A third mode, approving — anumodanā — is not undertaken in this two-way form.)
A 'land of action' where spiritual effort and liberation are possible; tradition counts fifteen such lands.
The fifteen occupations/trades a conscientious layperson avoids because they entail heavy harm to life (e.g. trades in fire, forests, ivory, poison, animals).
The four corrosive passions — krodha (anger), māna (pride), māyā (deceit) and lobha (greed) — items six through nine, which stain the soul and prolong bondage.

Lit. 'abandonment of the body'; the practice of standing or sitting motionless, detached from bodily concerns, while meditating — the fifth Āvaśyaka. It is the act this sūtra introduces, and its duration is counted in Logassa recitations.
Absolute, all-knowing omniscience attained on the path to liberation; in v.10 likened to a sun beside which the ordinary sun is a firefly.
Stainless, all-encompassing omniscience attained on the destruction of the ghāti karmas (v.4).
One possessing kevala-jñāna, omniscient infinite knowledge; every Tīrthaṅkara is a kevalin.
Asking-of-forgiveness; the act (and the song) of seeking pardon and releasing resentment.
Mutual forgiveness: asking pardon of all beings for harm done and granting pardon in return, so no enmity (vaira) remains.
Breaking or breach — failure to keep a rule of conduct intact.
A defensive moat or trench; here a moat of glowing khadira (acacia) embers encircling the fortress.
Praise, extolling; the verb kittaïssaṁ (v.1) is the reciter's vow 'I will praise'.
The three modes of agency — doing a deed oneself (sevyuṁ), causing another to do it (sevarāvyuṁ), and approving another's doing of it (anumodyuṁ) — all of which are confessed.
The Sanskrit term for mutual forgiveness, the spiritual heart of pratikramana.
'Forgiveness-ascetic' — an honorific for the guru/monk, addressed in vocative as khamāsamaṇo.
Place / region; the spatial boundary within which the vow is kept (inside the doorway of the upāśraya or present place).
A supernormal power or attainment arising from spiritual practice; Gautamasvāmī is called a 'treasury of labdhis'.
The 'color' or tint of the soul's mental disposition; the three dark ones — kṛṣṇa (black), nīla (blue), kāpota (grey) — are renounced.
'In the world / loka'; widens the fifth bow to embrace all true monks everywhere.
'Supreme in the world' — opening epithet of the 'loga' cluster (v.5): supreme in, lord of, benefactor of, lamp of, and illuminator of the world.
Pride or intoxicating conceit, one of the faults Vīra has wholly transcended (v.3).
Honey-bee / honey-maker; verse 5 calls the wise 'madhukara-samā', bee-like.
The 'bee-like' rule of alms-seeking: a little from many homes, harming none — the conduct this chapter enjoins.
'great fear / great peril' — the life-threatening dangers (disease, drowning, fire, snakebite, etc.) catalogued through the hymn.
A 'great satī' — a woman of spotless conduct, chastity, and spiritual steadfastness; the women honoured in vv.8–13.
A 'great being' — a soul of supreme courage and virtue; the men/monks honoured in vv.1–7.
A greater or weightier compulsion overriding the vow — an āgāra (mahattara-gāreṇaṁ).
The great central continent-region where Jinas are always present; here its five sections each yield four Jinas.
The 24th and final Tīrthaṅkara of this era; here hailed as world-teacher (jagaguru) and 'source of all scripture' (suyāṇaṁ pabhavo).
The five great vows of an ascetic: non-violence, truth, non-stealing, celibacy, non-possession.
Unchastity; sexual misconduct.
Spider-webs (literally 'spiders' threads'), one of the things one may inadvertently disturb while moving.
The three instruments of all action — mind, speech, and body — through which the renunciation operates.
The cosmic world-mountain; the Saṅgha as immovable, adamantine-based, golden-peaked (vv.12–18).
An auspicious benediction; the closing 'Sarvamaṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ…' seals the recitation with praise of the Jina-śāsana.
An auspicious invocation placed at the outset of a rite or text; the Navkar serves this role opening the Devasi pratikramaṇa.
a sacred efficacious formula; the hymn treats Pārśva's name-syllables as a mantra and conceals a special one within itself.
'King of mantras' — the supreme seed-mantra of Pārśva that the verse says must awaken, embodied, in the practitioner's heart.
Humans; 14 lakh birth-forms ('manuṣyanā bheda').
Epidemic or pestilence; listed among afflictions calmed by the mantra.
Deceitful falsehood; lying compounded with cunning intent to mislead.
The cosmic axis-mountain on whose summit the Indras ceremonially bathe a new-born Tīrthaṅkara (v.2).
'May the misdeed be void/vain' — the formula renouncing a wrongful act.
'May my wrong-doing be in vain' — the formula of confession that retracts and atones for the fault, here sealing the whole contemplation.
'May my wrongdoing be in vain / may it come to nothing' — the standard Jain formula of confession and seeking forgiveness that seals each section.
Wrong belief; the opposite of samyaktva. The text lists a four-fold mithyātva (worldly/transcendent, regarding deva and guru).
The 'thorn' (śalya) of wrong belief; deluded or false view of reality — the eighteenth and most fundamental source of sin, since it underlies the others.
Delusion/deluding karma; its absence marks the Jina's perfect insight.
Deluding karma; here in its faith-deluding forms — samyaktva-, miśra-, and mithyātva-mohanīya.
False speech; lying.
The small mouth-cloth inspected and held during the rite to guard subtle aerial life and speech.
A traditional unit of time (about 48 minutes); here it marks the minimum span of the vow.
A Jain monk; a renunciant who has taken the great vows.
The subtle inner sound with which the yogi merges; the verse likens absorption in the mantra to a yogi's union with nāda (v.10).
Declared cessation/abandonment of sinful activity, undertaken on entering the act of homage (nisīhiāe).
'Name-taking'; the act of reciting the names of the virtuous as remembrance and inspiration — the organizing device of this sutra.
Reverent salutation; here the Pañca-Namaskāra / Navkāra mantra whose lines are placed over the body.
'Reverence / I bow to'; the act of homage that opens each of the first five lines.
'Reverence be (to…)' — the opening words and common name of this sūtra; from Sanskrit namo'stu.
Joy, felicity; the prayer of vv.35–37, punning on the poet Nandiṣeṇa's own name.
The 'nine padas' — the five salutation-lines plus the four concluding lines (Eso pañca…, Savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo…, Maṅgalāṇaṁ…, Paḍhamaṁ hoi maṅgalaṁ) that make up the full Navkāra.
The ninefold safeguard of celibacy — nine rules protecting chastity in thought, word and deed.
The rosary used for counting Navkar recitations, named among the permitted possessions (dravya).
Self-censure — inwardly condemning one's own past fault; 'nindāmi' = 'I censure (it within myself).'
'I censure (myself)' — inward self-criticism of one's faults.
The 'stainless' liberated state; the final abode (dhāma) the two Jinas reach in v.14.
Striking down, destruction, eradication — here the destruction of accumulated sinful karmas, stated as the purpose (-aṭṭhāe = arthe, 'for the sake of') of the kāyotsarga.
Final liberation, here imaged as the 'city of nirvāṇa' the Jinas reach (v.5).
Unattached, free of all clinging; an epithet of the liberated Mahāvīra (v.3).
The specific personal limit or resolve a layperson sets within a vow (e.g. the exact extent of possessions or directions permitted).
'I renounce / I abstain' — the verb of renunciation that enacts the vow.
Ācārya Pālitta/Pādalipta, named in v.6 as one who greatly honoured Vīra; sometimes linked to the hymn's authorship (see notes).
The alms-bowl carried by a monk.
'I turn back / I repent' — withdrawal from transgression, the root act of pratikramaṇa.
'Turning back' — the act of reflecting on and atoning for a transgression; here the reciter's resolve to repent (paḍikkamiuṁ).
The yakṣiṇī (female guardian deity) of Pārśvanātha, worshipped alongside Dharaṇendra.
Tale-bearing or backbiting (Gujarati 'chāḍī'); secretly carrying others' faults to cause discord.
The Pākṣika (fortnightly) pratikramaṇa, within which this smaraṇa is recited; also the rite from which Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī derive by word-substitution.
Moss, mildew, or fungus — a one-sensed plant-body life-form easily crushed.
The five auspicious life-events of every Tīrthaṅkara: conception, birth, renunciation, omniscience, and liberation — the narrative spine of this hymn.
The five great vows of a mendicant (non-violence, truth, non-stealing, celibacy, non-possession); the firm pericarp of the Saṅgha-lotus.
'The fivefold salutation'; the collective name for the five bows of vv.1–5, referenced in v.6.
The five supreme beings worthy of worship: Arihanta, Siddha, Ācārya, Upādhyāya, and Sādhu — the five saluted in the Navkar.
'Five limbs' — the five-fold protective placement (nyāsa) of mantra-syllables on the body for guarding the meditator (v.7).
The five senses (touch, taste, smell, sight, hearing); the guru is 'sanvarano' — one who restrains them.
Lotus — emblem of Vīra's eyes and the ornament of water (jala).
Sin, demerit, karmic stain — what the reciter casts off in the closing refrain.
Sinful / demeritorious karma; the bondage that the practitioner aims to destroy (nigghāyaṇa) through this act (Prakrit pāvāṇaṁ kammāṇaṁ).
The 'bonds' or accumulated continuity of sin which, v.7 declares, dissolve at the utterance of these names.
A 'seat' or source of sin; one of the eighteen broad categories of conduct through which a soul incurs harmful (pāpa) karma.
Censuring or slandering others; finding fault with people behind their backs.
The five Supreme Beings — arihanta, siddha, ācārya, upādhyāya, sādhu — saluted in the Navkāra and invoked here for protection.
The formal act of releasing or 'breaking' a completed vow; here, the procedure for concluding the sāmāyika.
Possessiveness; attachment to possessions and accumulation.
'To forsake, renounce' — the verb governing the twenty-five faults to be abandoned.
the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara, to whom this hymn is addressed; identified by the serpent-hood and by Dharaṇendra's protection.
'I attend upon / abide in' — to dwell within the bound observance for the set period.
Devoted attendance / waiting upon; 'pajjuvāsāmi' = 'I attend upon (this state),' i.e. I remain steadfast in equanimity for the vow's duration.
The 'seat' of pontifical headship; one position in a teacher-to-disciple succession.
'Seat after seat'; the phrase marking continuous succession from one ācārya to the next.
A pontifical succession-list recording who held the teacher's seat (paṭṭa) after whom.
Lotus — the Saṅgha risen pure above the floodwaters of karmic dust (vv.7–8).
Sanskrit pāpa; sin, demerit, harmful karma. 'Savva-pāva-ppaṇāsaṇa' = destroyer of all sin.
A closing 'fruit' verse stating the benefit of the recitation; here vv.7 and 13 seal the two panels of the litany.
The 'declaration of fruit' — the verse(s) stating the benefits of reciting the hymn; here v.16, promising well-being to those who recite it on the holy days.
The 'statement of fruits' — verses (38–39) naming when the hymn is recited and the benefits (freedom from disease) it confers.
The closing 'statement of fruit' (v8) declaring the reward of the recitation — freedom from fear, disease, and affliction.
A lid or covering; the diamond roof sealing the fortress from above (v6).
Alms-food gathered as a 'lump'; nāṇā-piṇḍa = devoted to varied alms collected from many homes.
fainting or swoon caused by (a disturbance of) bile.
A periodic fast-and-restraint observance kept on holy days (parva).
Acts that illuminate and glorify the tīrtha / Jain order, raising its esteem in the world.
Reverential obeisance or bow; v.3 declares even a single bow to Pārśva 'highly fruitful'.
Injury to or destruction of life; violence — the first and gravest source of sin.
Gracious favour; the optative pasīyantu (v.5), 'may they be gracious to me'.
A laudatory or eulogistic verse; verse 5 is a praśasti proclaiming the victory of the Jaina śāsana.
The ritual of 'turning back' — reflection, confession, atonement and renewal that returns a practitioner from transgression to right conduct.
The mindful visual-and-tactile inspection of cloth and body for any living being before use; the act this formula accompanies.
A formal renunciation or vow taken for a coming period; the sixth Āvaśyaka. Here it frames the sāmāyika undertaken for the deśāvagāśika.
Renunciation or vow-resolution — the sixth Āvaśyaka, under which the sāmāyika is taken and concluded.
Atonement or expiation; the prescribed corrective penance by which a fault is repaired (Prakrit pāyacchitta).
Classes of malevolent spirits and ghosts that the Name and mantra are said to drive away (vv.3, 13).
Earth-bodied beings — life embodied in earth and minerals.
'Highest among men' — first of the four 'purisa' (best-of-men) epithets in verse 4, alongside lion, white-lotus, and fragrant-elephant of men.
Flower/blossom (Prakrit puppha), the source of the bee's nectar.
Attachment; the three named are kāma- (sensual), sneha- (affectional), and dṛṣṭi- (dogmatic) attachment.
Attachment and aversion — the two passions the practitioner resolves to remain free of during the vow.
The woolen broom a monk uses to gently sweep aside living beings; an emblem of ahiṃsā.
The ceremonial chariot-procession of the Jina image.
Indulgent delight and discontented aversion — taking pleasure in the sinful and displeasure in the wholesome.
The 'three jewels' — right knowledge (jñāna), right faith (darśana), and right conduct (cāritra) — to be adopted; their harm is virādhanā.
The daily pre-dawn reflection and atonement for the faults of the night, within which this sajjhāy is recited.
'Jewel-casket' — the simile for a teacher who keeps the anuyoga safe, as a casket guards gems (applied to Ārya Rakṣita).
The six daily obligatory observances in which the devotee is to be diligent each day.
Compassion toward the six classes of living beings (earth-, water-, fire-, air-, plant-bodied, and mobile beings).
The six classes of living beings — earth-, water-, fire-, air-bodied, plant life, and mobile (trasa) beings — toward all of whom forgiveness is sought.
The six classes of embodied life: earth-bodied (pṛthvīkāya), water-bodied (apkāya), fire-bodied (teukāya), air-bodied (vāukāya), plant-bodied (vanaspatikāya), and mobile (traskāya) beings.
Common-bodied vegetable life in which innumerable souls share a single body; the most numerous single class (14 lakh).
Affectionate service and support toward co-religionists who share one's faith.
An ascetic striving on the path; the fifth Parameṣṭhin, honoured with the others in the Navkar though not given a separate verse here.
A sudden or reflexive act done without forethought — an āgāra (sahasā-gāreṇaṁ).
Sanskrit sādhu; a Jain monk (and by extension ascetic) following the great vows; 'savva-sāhū' = all such monks.
Svādhyāya, sacred study-recitation; the genre in which this chapter is recited (not one of the six Āvaśyakas).
Sacred study-recitation, an internal austerity; the closing study-portion of the pratikramaṇa, distinct from the six Āvaśyakas.
Sacred-study recitation (Skt. svādhyāya); a hymn meant for reflective study, not one of the six Āvaśyakas.
Sacred-study recitation performed in the closing study-portion of a pratikramaṇa; not one of the six Āvaśyakas.
'Praise (stava) of Śakra' — the hymn of homage to the Arihantas traditionally spoken by Śakra/Indra; also called Namutthuṇaṁ or the Praṇipāta-sūtra.
The voluntary, peaceful fast unto death undertaken with equanimity at life's end; its 'final' (apaścima) form has its own five atichāras of lingering desire.
An inner 'thorn' obstructing liberation: māyā- (deceit), nidāna- (binding desire for reward), and mithyādarśana- (false faith) śalya.
Composed, undisturbed equanimity of mind; 'the supreme excellent samādhi' sought in v.6.
The divinely fashioned preaching assembly-hall in which a Tīrthaṅkara delivers his first sermon after attaining omniscience.
The state and vow of equanimity — a set period (traditionally one muhūrta, ~48 minutes) of withdrawing from worldly activity to rest in evenness of mind; the first of the six Āvaśyakas.
a slight movement or stirring; here of the limbs (aṅga), of phlegm (khela), and of the sight/eyes (diṭṭhi).
Carefulness — the five rules of vigilant conduct (in walking, speech, seeking food, handling things, and disposal) that prevent harm to living beings.
The voluntary, gradual ritual fast undertaken at life's end; its five atichārs (e.g. wishing for this world or the next) are examined in v.13.
a hymn of sustained praise; the poem calls itself a saṁstava that destroys great fear.
Stopping the inflow of karma through restraint.
Restraint or stopping of the inflow of karma; here, holding the senses in check.
Right faith/insight (Prakrit sammatta) — one of the three jewels; imaged as the city's streets and the moon's moonlight.

Right faith / correct world-view; affirmed at the outset because it is the root that gives the vows their value. Here called samakita; its own five atichārs include doubt (śaṅkā) and craving (kāṅkṣā).
Self-restraint/discipline; paired with tapa as the hub of the Saṅgha-wheel and the moon's spotless mark.
Ornament, adornment — the epithet given to each teacher as the glory of his gaccha.
The four-fold Jain community — monks (sādhu), nuns (sādhvī), laymen (śrāvaka), laywomen (śrāvikā); the central object of praise in this hymn.
Contact or brushing-against; here, inadvertent physical touch of beings or persons while moving.
'Peace'; Śāntinātha, the 16th Tīrthaṅkara — and, by word-play, the peace the devotee seeks.
The 16th Tīrthaṅkara; born at Hastināpura (Gajapura) in the Viśvasena line, with the deer (antelope) as emblem; also a universal monarch (cakravartin).
Refuge; the act of taking shelter in the Jinas (v.6, 'saraṇaṁ pavajjaha').
'All-knowing'; paired with sarvadarśin, 'all-seeing' (savvadarisī), in verse 10.
The teaching/dispensation of a Jina; v.24 proclaims the triumph of Vīra's śāsana, which destroys false doctrine (kusamaya).
Eternal (ever-existing) versus non-eternal (made in time) — a distinction applied to Jina-temples and images.
The four eternal Jinas — Ṛṣabhānana, Candrānana, Vardhamāna, Vāriṣeṇa — whose images are held to exist perpetually.
An eternal, beginningless Jina image enshrined in the heavens and cosmic mountains, contrasted with historically founded (non-eternal) shrines.
The six classes of embodied life — earth-, water-, fire-, air-, plant-, and mobile-bodied beings — whose injury (virādhanā) is confessed.
Good fortune and well-being; one of the worldly boons promised to the devotee (v.7).
Sinful or blameworthy activity — any act of mind, speech, or body that causes harm; what the practitioner renounces for the vow's duration.
'For the sake of complete equanimity/well-being' — the exception allowing one to act to preserve overall calm or health.
A fully liberated, bodiless soul that has shed all karma and dwells in perfection at the summit of the universe; the second Parameṣṭhin (v.2).
Accomplishment or attainment — both worldly success and, ultimately, spiritual fulfilment; the hymn closes promising 'all siddhi' (vv.11, 14).

The 'destination of perfection' — the abode of the liberated, qualified in v.10 by seven epithets: śiva (blissful), acala (immovable), aruja (free of disease), ananta (endless), akṣaya (imperishable), avyābādha (untroubled), apunarāvṛtti (no return to rebirth).
A 'training vow'; the last four of the twelve lay vows, of which Deśāvagāśika is one, that school the layperson toward stricter conduct.
Right conduct / chastity; the defining virtue of the satīs, called akalaṅka ('stainless') in v.13.
The 'limbs of conduct'; the eighteen-thousand (aṭhāra-sahassa) combinations of monastic discipline.
A 'remembrance' — a short protective stotra recited from memory; this is the sixth of the nine such Smaraṇas in the Pākṣika pratikramaṇa.
A striving Jain ascetic / monk (Prakrit samaṇa).
A Jain layperson (householder devotee); the feminine is śrāvikā. The subject who undertakes and reviews these vows.
Lay devotee, male/female; honoured with 21 gunas.
The 'jewel of scripture' — sacred knowledge, imaged as the city's treasure and the lotus's stalk.
A tāntric act of 'immobilizing' or arresting — here halting approaching karmic afflictions ('stambhaya stambhaya svāhā', v.4).
A hymn of praise to a Jina; here, praise of Vīra (hence Vīra-stava).
A symbolic representation of the guru (often a small ritual stand) established so the rite is performed in the guru's presence.
An elder; a senior monk-teacher who holds and transmits the tradition.
The roll or chain of elders — the ordered succession of teachers, named verse by verse in this sajjhāy.
A celebrated early ācārya renowned for mastery over the passions; named among the auspicious in verse 1.
A verse of praise or hymn of homage; the genre (kind) of this sutra.
The instruction of a true, qualified teacher, under which all these duties are to be performed.
subtle, very slight — qualifying the barely perceptible stirrings that are excused.
Sacred self-study and recitation of scripture; the ritual category to which this sajjhāy belongs (not one of the six Āvaśyakas).
The ritual sealing-exclamation; the 'svāhā-ending pada' caps and empowers the protective formula.
'Self-awakened' — one who attains enlightenment by himself, without instruction from another (Prakrit sayaṁsaṁbuddha).
Austerity/ascetic practice; its 'fierce rays' make the Saṅgha-sun outshine rival doctrines.
Here the 'essence/reality' that is meditated upon — the coloured mantra-form held in the lotus that yields all results (vv.5–7).
Tapas — austerity, ascetic discipline (Prakrit tava).
Fire-bodied beings — life embodied in fire.
Fire-bodied beings (one-sensed); 7 lakh forms.
by steady posture, by silence, and by meditation — the three means by which the body is 'set aside.'
A 'crossing-place' — a sacred Jain pilgrimage site; figuratively, the means of crossing the ocean of rebirth. The hymn bows to 108 of them.
A holy pilgrimage place / a 'garland' listing many such places, as in dhāla 8 (Śatruñjaya, Girnār, Sammeta-śikhara, etc.).
Pilgrimage to the holy crossing-places (tīrthas).
A 'garland of tīrthas' — a poetic genre that strings sacred sites together as a verbal pilgrimage.
A 'ford-maker', a fully awakened Jina who re-establishes the fourfold community and the path; twenty-four arise in each half time-cycle.
Animals (non-human five-sensed beings); 4 lakh forms in this enumeration.
'By the three-fold means' — i.e. with mind, speech, and body; the manner of the closing salute to all Siddhas (v.11).
Mobile beings — those able to move of their own accord (insects, animals, humans).
The 'three worlds' (upper, middle, lower) across which the satīs' fame is said to sound.
Threefold homage offered for the past, present, and future — the devotional refrain that paces the closing salutations.
The three worlds — adho-loka (lower), ūrdhva-loka (upper) and tiryag-loka (middle) — the framework of dhālas 5–7.
The preceptor who teaches the scriptures (śruta) to the order; the fourth Parameṣṭhin (v.4).
calamity or disturbance; v.24 says all upadravas are destroyed by Pārśva's power.
An affliction or attack — whether by humans, animals, or supernatural beings — endured by an ascetic (v.4) and dispelled for the devotee (v.6).
The hall / place of religious observance whose threshold defines the spatial (kṣetra) limit of the vow.
breathing out / breathing in — the most basic permitted reflexes.
'Carrying further / higher refinement' — performing an additional act that completes and elevates the confession just made, so that nothing left over goes unaddressed.
Small ground-insects or ant-hills (variant glosses exist); included among things trodden underfoot.
Sanskrit upādhyāya; the teaching-monk responsible for instructing other ascetics in the scriptures.
A 'reader/reciter' — an ācārya authorized to expound the scriptures; hence vācaka-vaṁśa, the lineage of such expounders.
Vajrasvāmī ('Vayar'), an early ācārya celebrated for his vidyās and labdhis; remembered in verse 2.
'Diamond cage' — the adamantine, impenetrable protective enclosure the reciter mentally builds; the hymn's title image.
Plant-bodied beings, divided into pratyeka (individual-souled, 10 lakh) and sādhāraṇa (common-bodied / nigoda, 14 lakh).
Veneration of the guru; the third of the six Avashyakas, under which this sutra falls.
Reverential salutation/veneration; the Third of the six daily obligations (Āvaśyaka), directed to the guru.
Rampart or fortress wall; in v5 the 'savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo' line becomes the outer adamantine rampart.
Mahāvīra's given name, meaning 'the increasing/ever-growing one' (Prakrit Vaddhamāṇa).
The year-long giving of gifts a Tīrthaṅkara distributes before renouncing the world (v.9).
Air-bodied beings — life embodied in air/wind.
Air-bodied beings (one-sensed); 7 lakh forms.
A malign spirit/zombie-demon, one of the terrors listed among the upasargas dispelled in verse 6.
The prescribed correct procedure (vidhi) and any lapse from it (avidhi); the conclusion confesses any avidhi committed.
A procedural ritual text (a declaration or stage-direction) as opposed to a verse-sūtra given the full word-by-word treatment.
The self-designation of the Anchalgachchha (Achalagaccha) in this book; literally the 'order of [correct] procedure.'
The 'party of [correct] procedure' — the self-designation of the Anchalgachchha.
Bird, a sky-goer; the free, unpossessive flyer to which the mendicant is also likened (v.3).
The 'wandering', currently-living Tīrthaṅkaras — the 20 Jinas presently teaching in the Mahāvideha regions, led by Sīmandhara.
The twenty Tīrthaṅkaras presently living and teaching in other regions (notably Sīmandhara-svāmī), distinct from the twenty-four of our era.
'Having shaken off the rust-stain (of karma)'; the epithet in v.5 imaging liberation as cleansed metal.
Mahāvīra, the 24th and last Tīrthaṅkara of the present era; here hailed as auspicious.
Injury to or violation of living beings or of one's vows.
Injury, violation, or harm done to living beings, whether deliberate or incidental.
a venom-bearer, i.e. a serpent (Prakrit visahara); the danger of snakebite faced in vv.8–9 and listed in v.18.
'Poison-remover/snake-related'; the name of the protective mantra, and an epithet evoking Pārśva's power over poison and serpents.

'De-thorning' — the drawing-out of the śalya, the inner darts lodged in the soul (classically the three: māyā/deceit, nidāna/craving for reward, mithyātva/false belief), so that no concealed barb obstructs progress (Prakrit visallīkaraṇa).
Cleansing or purification of the soul from the stain left by transgression (Prakrit visohī).
'I renounce / I give it up' — the concluding act of relinquishment that seals a pratyākhyāna.
'I abandon / give up' — the operative verb of renunciation, here of attachment to the body.
A vow or disciplined commitment undertaken by a practitioner.
Casting off / abandonment; 'appāṇaṁ vosirāmi' = 'I cast off my (faulty) self,' renouncing the self conditioned by past blameworthy activity.
Renunciation/casting-off; 'appāṇaṁ vosirāmi' — I abandon the (sinful) self/act, the closing surrender.
A Sanskrit poetic figure of repeated syllable-sequences with differing sense; pervasive in this hymn and central to its musical effect, alongside anuprāsa (alliteration).
A pair or twin; here the device of worshipping the two Jinas Ajita and Śānti together as one inseparable unit.
Sustainable capacity; used both for bowing 'to the limit of my capacity' and for asking whether the guru's body and senses are sustaining without affliction.
The 'drum of fame'; the metaphor of v.13 — the renown of spotless śīla still resounds throughout the worlds.
Food 'as already prepared' for the householder's own use (Prakrit ahākaḍa), which alone the monk may accept — faultless alms.
'According to one's capacity'; the principle that one vows honestly to what one can actually sustain.
Spiritual 'journey' — the guru's continued restraint and ascetic endeavour, inquired after in 'jattā bhe?'.
The pre-eminent teacher of an age, the foremost authority of his generation.
'Foremost of the age'; an epithet for a supremely authoritative teacher, applied here to Āryarakṣitasūri.
